Article Title: Not just Japan: Rui Hachimura wants Asia's basketball dream to grow bigger
Article Text:
Rui Hachimura (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

Thinking beyond Japan

NBA stars under one roof (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

The challenge remains bigger than talent



Asia's best talents in action (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

Building pathways

Lauren Jackson (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

Mitch Richmond (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

A tournament and a question

The lone Indian representatives (Photo by NBA Rising Stars Invitational)

TimesofIndia.com in Singapore: By mid-morning, the courts inside the OCBC Arena had already settled into their rhythm.On one end, South Korea's Kyungbock High School looked every bit the early favourites, their players physically bigger and noticeably more imposing than most of their opponents. Singapore, with four teams spread across the boys' and girls' categories, found the going tougher against more established basketball programmes.Around the arena, coaches barked instructions, and teenagers from across Asia shared courts that, for the next week, would become the centre of the NBA 's ambitions in the region.The facilities are first class, the organisation unmistakably NBA. But what stood out most on the opening day of the Rising Stars Invitational wasn't necessarily the basketball itself.It was the diversity. Players from Japan, China, South Korea, India, the Philippines and Australia, each carrying different basketball traditions and ambitions, all arriving in Singapore with the same dream.For some, it is about winning. For others, simply being here is part of something bigger: Opportunity.For Rui Hachimura, perhaps more than anybody else, that word carries special meaning.The opportunity he wished he had.The Los Angeles Lakers forward is not just Japan's biggest basketball export. In 2019, Hachimura became the first Japanese player to be selected in the first round of the NBA Draft when the Washington Wizards picked him ninth overall.The 27-year-old, who won three consecutive national high school championships before moving to Gonzaga University, remains the only active NBA player from Asia and has increasingly embraced the responsibility that comes with it."You know, I think when I was a kid, I always want to play overseas," Hachimura said on the sidelines of the event."I think this kind of opportunity is very important. I don't know how long this has existed, but when I was in high school, I don't think it existed. So that's why it makes sense."Looking around the arena and watching teenagers from across Asia compete under the NBA banner, Hachimura admitted he recognised a younger version of himself."Yeah, of course," he said when asked whether he saw himself in these players."When I was in high school, I got invited [to the] like [of] Jordan Brand Classic, NBA Academy and all that. But those are times that I actually go and learn myself. I can see my level and my goal.""So these kind of stuff is going to help them. Especially in Asia, there are a lot of kids, a lot of players that want to be in the NBA. So this is really good stuff."And perhaps that was the sentiment echoed most frequently throughout the day: Pathways.For years, Asian basketball has searched for figures capable of changing perceptions.China had Yao Ming, an eight-time NBA All-Star and the first overall pick in the 2002 Draft. Japan found its breakthrough star in Hachimura, who has since been joined by Yuta Watanabe and Yuki Kawamura in carrying the country's basketball aspirations.But for Hachimura himself, the conversation has moved beyond national boundaries."Especially for me, I think I have a responsibility. My country Japan, but also the whole Asia," he said."Right now, literally I'm the only NBA player in Asia. So that's the tell that we need to grow our basketball culture and bring more Asian players into the NBA.""I think we have a lot of potential, but I think there are few opportunities. That's why I decided to come here and try to help the whole Asia continent to bring more players to the NBA."Later, when asked what success for Asian basketball would look like a decade from now, Hachimura again returned to the same theme."I don't want to talk about Japan," he said, adding."I think about whole Asia. That's my vision.""I want to inspire those kids and I want to have more kids from Asia be NBA players and do their thing. I'm thinking how I can help with that."For Hachimura, physical disadvantages are no longer excuses. "There is always this disadvantage for us," he said."But I think there are a lot of ways to prove that. You guys see Yuki Kawamura. He's literally this height and he competed every day, every game.""You just got to believe in yourself and find a way."More interestingly, Hachimura believes the challenge is not purely physical."It's the personality too," he said. "Basketball is an interesting sport. It's a team sport but a lot of times it's individual too. Showing what you can do is really important.""That's the things that I had to learn when I got to Gonzaga [University]. They always told me I play like I'm shy or something.""But you can't be like that. You always got to be aggressive. You got to be the guy trying to change the whole game. I think the Asians don't have that, so I think I have to teach that."Three-time WNBA MVP Lauren Jackson, widely regarded as one of the greatest players in the history of women's basketball and a central figure in Australia's rise as a global force, believes opportunity is becoming the defining theme of this generation.A Basketball Hall of Famer and one of the faces of the NBA's Her Time To Play initiative, Jackson has seen the women's game evolve over more than two decades."I think women's basketball is driving it globally," Jackson said. "I think there's no doubt that the WNBA has definitely lifted the stakes.""We're seeing the recognition because of women's basketball and it's such a great time for women's sport."For Jackson, tournaments like the Rising Stars Invitational are important because they create visibility."This tournament creates an opportunity for all the other countries to really have a pathway to the elite," she said."They can see their way to the NBA and that's something that's never been done before, particularly in the Asia-Pacific."Mitch Richmond, a six-time NBA All-Star, 2002 NBA champion with the Los Angeles Lakers and a member of the Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame, has witnessed the league's international expansion from close quarters.Part of the famed trio at Golden State Warriors alongside Tim Hardaway and Chris Mullin, Richmond believes the NBA's efforts to globalise the game have been decades in the making."I saw it coming," Richmond said of basketball's global growth."When I was playing, the NBA always had me go overseas and they were really trying to expand.""To see it all come to fruition right now, where the NBA is growing in so many places and continues to grow globally, it's really a sight to see."And when asked about countries like India, where cricket remains dominant, Richmond preferred patience over urgency."You have to start somewhere. You have to take those baby steps. It's about teaching and learning. You have to get the youth excited."The lone Indian representatives, Velammal International School, will begin their campaign on Tuesday.Their task is difficult. The gap in infrastructure and basketball culture between India and some of Asia's established powers remains evident.Yet, that is perhaps the larger significance of this week. Because for Hachimura, success is no longer measured by what Japan achieves."I have a bigger goal now," he said. "Not only Japan but whole Asia."Inside the OCBC Arena, surrounded by teenagers from across the region, that vision no longer feels entirely distant.And for countries still waiting for their breakthrough moment, that might be reason enough to keep believing.
Article Summary: Rui Hachimura is the first Japanese player to be selected in the first round of the NBA Draft. The 27-year-old remains the only active NBA player from Asia. He has increasingly embraced the responsibility that comes with it. He wants to inspire those kids and have more kids from Asia compete.

Article Title: Will Angel Reese attend FIFA World Cup game? Atlanta Dream star drops latest update
Article Text:
Angel Reese. Image via: Michael Reaves/ Getty Images

Will Angel Reese attend the FIFA World Cup game?



Angel Reese. Image via: Todd Kirkland/ Getty Images

Angel Reese appears in a stunning outfit before the Atlanta Dream game



Angel Reese. Image via: wnba/ Instagram

Angel Reese's performance this season so far



Will Angel Reese attend FIFA World Cup game? The Atlanta Dream star says she plans to be part of the tournament atmosphere in Atlanta after missing an opportunity to attend a match earlier this week, adding another chapter to a summer that has already featured major milestones on and off the court.Speaking on SportsCenter on Monday, Reese confirmed she intends to attend one of the World Cup matches at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. The update comes as the WNBA standout continues a strong first season with the Atlanta Dream, who entered Monday night's game against the Toronto Tempo riding a back-to-back winning streak.Angel Reese revealed that some of her Atlanta teammates were able to attend Sunday's World Cup match in the city, but her schedule prevented her from joining them."My teammates were able to go (Sunday). I didn't make it (Sunday), but I will plan to make one of the games," Reese said on SportsCenter.Her teammates watched Spain cruise to a 4-0 victory over Saudi Arabia at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. While Reese hopes to attend a future match, she specifically mentioned her desire to support the United States men's national team if the opportunity arises."If the U.S. comes here, I definitely want to see them play and go out there and support," she said."But it's really cool to see a different environment, different people coming here just to enjoy sports. It's because I really love sports."Although the current tournament bracket means the USMNT is not scheduled to play in Atlanta, Reese's comments highlighted the growing crossover between major international soccer events and American professional athletes. Atlanta has become one of the busiest host cities during the tournament, drawing large crowds and global attention.The 24-year-old also discussed a recent milestone that further strengthened her case as one of the league's premier rebounders. Reese became the fastest player in WNBA history to reach 1,000 career rebounds, accomplishing the feat in just 79 games."I think people don't realize, rebounding is harder than you think. Defensive rebounds (are) a little bit more easier, but going in and crashing every single time to get second-chance opportunities on the offensive end," Reese said."You can look statistically, but not just from my shots but from my teammates, and it gives us more points. It gives us more opportunities. Just being able to come out and do what I do every single night just shows my consistency."Before Monday's matchup against the Toronto Tempo, Reese once again drew attention with her pregame fashion choice.The two-time WNBA All-Star arrived at State Farm Arena wearing a floor-length bronze kaftan-style gown featuring a plunging V-neckline and textured pleated design. She paired the look with a black choker necklace, metallic strappy heels and cornrow braids.Reese's tunnel appearances have become a regular talking point throughout the season, reflecting her growing influence beyond basketball as one of the league's most recognizable personalities.While Reese continues to make headlines away from the court, her production remains the biggest reason Atlanta sits atop the Eastern Conference standings.The Dream forward is averaging a career-high 15.3 points, 11.9 rebounds, 2.7 assists and 1.3 steals per game. Her impact on both ends of the floor has helped Atlanta compile an 11-4 record and emerge as one of the WNBA's early championship contenders.
Article Summary: Angel Reese says she plans to attend one of the World Cup matches in Atlanta. Reese missed an opportunity to attend a match earlier this week. The 24-year-old is averaging a career-high 15.3 points, 11.9 rebounds and 2.7 assists.

Article Title: Knicks champion spotted making it rain at strip club after eight-day drinking pledge
Article Text:
Jose Alvarado. Image via: Gregory Shamus/ Getty Images

NBA title winner Jose Alvarado spotted partying at a strip club as celebrations continue



Jose Alvarado at at night club. Image via: Starlets NY/ Instagram

What is Jose Alvarado's future with the Knicks?



Knicks champion Jose Alvarado has become one of the faces of New York's NBA title celebrations after footage emerged of the guard throwing stacks of cash at a Queens strip club, extending the festivities tied to the franchise's first championship in more than five decades.The late-night appearance came less than two weeks after the New York Knicks secured the 2026 NBA Finals title. Alvarado, a midseason addition who quickly earned a key role under head coach Mike Brown, has embraced the spotlight during the city's championship celebrations while also facing an important contract decision that could shape his future with the organization.Jose Alvarado's championship tour took another turn when he was filmed at Starlets, a gentleman's club in Queens, tossing dollar bills into the air as fans gathered around the stage. Videos circulating online showed the Knicks guard wearing a heavy chain and taking part in a celebration that reflected the mood surrounding New York's long-awaited title victory.The outing followed comments Alvarado made during a podcast before the playoffs reached their conclusion."If we win, I'm going to be drunk for eight days," Alvarado said. "I'm having a party in like every state."Since the Knicks lifted the Larry O'Brien Trophy, Alvarado has seemingly remained committed to that promise.He has appeared at multiple parties and public celebrations across New York, becoming one of the most visible players during the city's championship festivities.Hours before the nightclub appearance, Alvarado posted a photo of himself asleep face-down on a folding table during his travels. The image carried the caption "Day 7," prompting many fans to assume the marathon celebration was winding down.Instead, the veteran guard found enough energy for another headline-grabbing stop.The Knicks' championship parade through Manhattan earlier in the week drew massive crowds as players, celebrities, team owner James Dolan and New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani celebrated the franchise's first title in 53 years. The parade highlighted how deeply the championship resonated across the city after decades of waiting.While the celebrations continue, business is already underway for the reigning champions.The Knicks hold the No. 24 and No. 31 picks in the 2026 NBA Draft and are actively exploring roster and salary-cap options. Alvarado's contract situation is one of several issues the front office must address before free agency begins.After arriving in a trade from the New Orleans Pelicans, Alvarado averaged 7.4 points, 3.4 assists and 2.5 rebounds while providing the defensive intensity and energy that helped strengthen New York's rotation during its championship run.The 27-year-old holds a $4.5 million player option for the 2026-27 season. Rather than making an immediate decision, Alvarado and the Knicks agreed to move the deadline from June 22 to June 26."While the reigning champion New York Knicks assess their trade options for picks No. 24 and 31 in this week's NBA Draft, sources say reserve guard Jose Alvarado has agreed to move the decision date on his $4.5 million player option for 2026-27 from tomorrow, June 22, back to June 26," NBA insider Jake Fischer reported in a post on X.The extra time gives both sides flexibility ahead of draft night, while leaving open the question of whether one of New York's most beloved championship contributors will return for another title defense.
Article Summary: Knicks guard Jose Alvarado spotted partying at a strip club as celebrations continue. The late-night appearance came less than two weeks after the New York Knicks secured the 2026 NBA Finals title. The 27-year-old guard holds a $4.5 million player option for the 20 26-27 season.
